segments.purgatorio-32-g51: 'Io' is the subject 'I'. 'non' negates the verb. 'lo' is the masculine direct-object pronoun 'it' referring to something just mentioned. '’ntesi' is an archaic contracted form of the past tense of 'ntendere' (modern 'intesi'): here first-person past, meaning 'I heard' (with the negative, 'I did not hear'). The initial vowel is elided in poetry (’n- instead of in-).
segments.purgatorio-32-g52: 'né' begins a second negative clause ('nor'). 'qui' is the adverb 'here' (in this place/part of the poem). 'non si canta' uses the impersonal/reflective 'si' plus present indicative to mean 'is sung' or 'one does not sing' (impersonal passive). 'l’ inno' = 'the hymn' (elision of 'il'). The relative clause 'che quella gente allor cantaro' modifies 'inno': 'che' = 'which/that', 'quella gente' = 'those people', 'allor' = 'then', and 'cantaro' is an archaic third-person plural past (passato remoto) of 'cantare' meaning 'they sang' (spelled in older form without the modern -no ending).
segments.purgatorio-32-g53: Again 'né' continues the negation ('nor'). 'la nota' is 'the note' or musical sound/timbre (direct object). 'soffersi' is first-person past of 'soffrire' (here 'I endured' / 'I could bear'), used reflexively/archaisms in Dante. 'tutta quanta' is an emphatic phrase meaning 'entirely' or 'the whole of it' (tutta = whole, quanta reinforcing 'as much as' = 'completely'). Word order places the object before the verb for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-32-g54: S’io = se io (if I); S’ is an elision of 'se' introducing a counterfactual condition. 'potessi' is the imperfect subjunctive (first person singular) used here for an unreal/remote condition ('if I could'). 'ritrar' is an infinitive meaning 'to portray/draw' and follows the modal sense of potessi. The clause then continues with a subordinate clause introduced by 'come' (how) where the verb 'assonnaro' (past tense) comes after the complement 'li occhi spietati' because Dante inverts word order for poetic rhythm: literally 'how became drowsy the pitiless eyes upon hearing the Syrinx.' 'udendo' is a gerund ('hearing') qualifying the circumstance, and 'di Siringa' names the cause/agent ('of the Syrinx' = the reed-pipe or its sound).
segments.purgatorio-32-g55: 'li occhi' repeats the subject ('the eyes'). 'a cui' is a relative phrase meaning 'for whom' or 'to whom'; here it links back to 'li occhi.' 'pur' is an adverb meaning 'even' or 'still.' 'vegghiar' (archaic infinitive) means 'to keep watch, to stay awake.' 'costò' is passato remoto (third person singular) 'cost'/'came to cost' and 'sì caro' ('so dearly') completes the notion: for those eyes, keeping awake had been very costly (i.e. difficult to maintain).
segments.purgatorio-32-g56: 'come pintor' begins a simile ('as a painter'). 'che' introduces a relative clause ('who'). 'con essempro' = 'with a model/pattern' (essempro = 'esempio/essemplo', an archaic spelling). 'pinga' is the present subjunctive (third person singular) of 'pingere' used in the relative simile ('who paints'); the subjunctive here gives a general, exemplary sense ('as a painter who, using a model, paints').
segments.purgatorio-32-g57: 'disegnerei' is the conditional present first person singular ('I would draw/depict'), and it completes the protasis–apodosis begun by 'S'io potessi'. 'com'' = 'come' shortened before a vowel and here means 'how.' 'io' is the subject pronoun. 'mddormentai' = 'mi addormentai' is passato remoto first person singular ('I fell asleep') with 'mi' as the reflexive clitic; the clause states what the speaker would depict (how he fell asleep).
segments.purgatorio-32-g58: 'ma' = 'but' introduces a concessive remark. 'qual' here is an indefinite ('whoever' / 'anyone'); 'vuol' is present indicative third person singular of 'volere' ('wants'/'is willing'). 'sia' is present subjunctive of 'essere' ('be') used in a clause of opinion/allowance ('let it be that ...'). The 'che' that follows introduces the content clause. 'lssonnar' is the infinitive used as a noun ('the drowsing/sleepiness' or 'the pretending to be asleep'), 'ben' = 'well', and 'finga' is the present subjunctive third person singular of 'fingere' ('feign/pretend'). The whole means roughly 'but let anyone who wishes suppose that the drowsiness was well feigned.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g59: 'Per�' (Per) = 'Per�' (Per�) = 'Per�' (here 'Per�' = 'therefore/nevertheless') introduces the transition. 'trascorro' is first person singular present; Dante uses it in the sense 'I pass over / I skip forward (in my account)'. 'a quando' = 'to the time when'; 'mi svegliai' is passato remoto first person singular ('I woke up'). The sentence means 'I pass over to the moment when I awoke.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g60: 'e dico' = 'and I say' (narrative present). 'ch' = 'that' introduces the content clause. 'un splendor' = 'a brightness/radiance.' 'mi squarci�' uses 'squarci�' (passato remoto, third person singular) with the dative 'mi' of reference: literally 'a brightness tore open for me the veil' — Dante often places the direct object 'l velo' after the verb; 'del sonno' ('of sleep') specifies that it was the veil of sleep that was torn.
segments.purgatorio-32-g61: 'e un chiamar' uses the infinitive 'chiamar' as a noun: 'and a calling' or 'and a cry.' The indefinite article 'un' makes it 'a cry' and it prepares the direct quotation that follows.
segments.purgatorio-32-g62: The word 'Surgi' is the second person singular imperative (or present form used as command) of 'sorgere' ('rise, get up'). In the direct speech it is a sharp command: 'Rise!'
segments.purgatorio-32-g63: The short question 'che fai?' is direct speech: 'che' as interrogative 'what' and 'fai' is present indicative second person singular of 'fare' ('do'). Together: 'what are you doing?' The colon/quotation marks in the text show this is the cry that awakened him.
segments.purgatorio-32-g64: This segment is a single punctuation mark (a period). It has no grammatical words or syntactic role beyond marking the end of a sentence or a pause in the text; in poetry it can signal a strong closure or editorial separation. There are no verb forms, particles, or morphological elements to analyze here.
segments.purgatorio-32-g1: Tant' is the clipped/telescoped form of tanto meaning 'so much.' Eran is the archaic imperfect of essere ('were') and with the following predicate adjectives (fissi e attenti) describes the state of the subject li occhi miei (the inverted order places the adjective phrase before the infinitive clause). a + infinitive (a disbramarsi) indicates purpose or intent ('in order to' / 'intent on'), here with the reflexive disbramarsi ('to rid oneself of, abate, or restrain desire') governing la decenne sete as its object. Decenne is an adjectival/poetic form meaning 'of ten years / tenfold', modifying sete ('thirst').
segments.purgatorio-32-g2: Che introduces the result clause ('so that' / 'that'), and the subject-li altri sensi ('the other senses') appears in the normal noun + article order. M’eran is the clitic mi + imperfect eran ('were to me' i.e. 'were for me' / 'were in me'), with tutti spenti as predicate (tutti 'all', spenti 'extinguished'). The construction says that, as a result of the eyes' intense focus, his other senses were effectively put out.
segments.purgatorio-32-g3: Ed = e ('and'); essi here refers back to the things previously described (plural 'they/those'), quinci e quindi is a paired locative/adverbial phrase 'on this side and on that' or 'from here and from there.' Avien is a literary imperfect of avere ('they had'); parete (from paretere) functions idiomatically with avien to mean 'they had the appearance' or 'they seemed' (lit. 'they had seeming/appearance') followed by di non caler, where di introduces the subordinate infinitive clause. Così introduces the result 'thus,' and lo santo riso is 'the holy smile' as agent; a sé traéli con l’antica rete = 'drew them to itself among them with the ancient net' (traéli = among them; rete = net/snare used metaphorically).
segments.purgatorio-32-g4: This segment is a punctuation mark (an em-dash / long pause) used in the verse to break the thought; no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-32-g5: Quando introduces a temporal clause 'when.' Per forza is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by force' or 'with force.' Mi fu vòlto is a perfect passive/periphrastic construction (mi = clitic 'to me' or 'my face' as indirect object, fu = auxiliary 'was', vòlto = past participle 'turned'), so 'my face was turned (toward)...' Ver' is a poetic contraction of verso 'toward'; la sinistra mia = 'my left (side)'; da quelle dee = 'by those dee' — here dee is the plural of dea (goddess) in the poem's diction, i.e. 'those goddesses' (agent performing the turning).
segments.purgatorio-32-g6: Perch' is an apocopated perché meaning 'because' or 'for.' Io udi' is the perfect 'I heard' (ici with final apostrophe marking dropped vowel). Da loro = 'from them'; un «Troppo fiso!» is an exclamatory phrase they uttered — Troppo 'too/overly' + fiso (from fisso) 'fixed/intently' — an admonition meaning roughly 'too fixed!' or 'you are staring too intently.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g7: This segment is a semicolon in the verse; it separates clauses and has no lexical content to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-32-g8: E = 'and.' La disposizion ('the disposition' or 'readiness/aptitude') is followed by a relative clause ch’a veder èe where ch' = che ('that/which') and a + infinitive (a veder) expresses purpose or the type of disposition ('for seeing' / 'to see'); èe is an archaic/poetic variant of è ('is'). Ne li occhi = 'in the eyes' (ne = in/of them), pur testé = 'even/still just now' (pur = still/yet; testé = just now/recently), dal sol percossi = 'struck by the sun' (dal = da + il 'by/from the', sol = poetic form for 'sun', percossi = past participle 'struck/affected'). The whole clause says the disposition for seeing had been impressed in the eyes by the recent action of the sun.
segments.purgatorio-32-g9: Sanza = senza 'without.' La vista = 'the sight/vision'; alquanto = 'somewhat' / 'a little'; esser mi fée is an older/poetic ordering meaning literally 'to be made to me' (esser + mi + fée/fece), i.e. 'my sight being somewhat made/restored' — the negative sansa says this restoration did not occur. The final fée is a poetic form (here functioning as the past action 'made' or 'caused'), not the modern sense 'fairy.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g86: Qui is an adverb meaning “here,” setting location. Sarai is the future indicative (2nd person singular) of essere, with the subject tu (here stated for emphasis) — sarai tu = “you will be.” Poco tempo is an adverbial phrase “for a short time” that modifies sarai. Silvano is a predicative adjective/noun meaning “sylvan, wood-dweller” (an archaic/poetic term); word order is inverted for poetic effect (verb before subject and predicative).
segments.purgatorio-32-g87: E is the simple coordinating conjunction linking to what follows. Sarai is again future indicative 2nd singular (elliptical subject tu). Meco is the archaic adverbial form meaning “with me” placed after the verb (poetic order). Sanza fine is a phrase “without end” = “everlasting/eternal” modifying cive. Cive is a predicative noun meaning “citizen/inhabitant.” Di quella Roma is a prepositional phrase specifying which city (di + demonstrative + proper name). Onde introduces a relative clause “from/whence” and Cristo is its subject; è is present indicative of essere; romano is a predicative adjective “of Rome/Roman.” Note the archaic forms (meco, cive, sanza) and the relative onde linking the clause.
segments.purgatorio-32-g88: Però is a linking adverb meaning “therefore/then,” signaling consequence or purpose and connecting this instruction to what preceded.
segments.purgatorio-32-g89: In pro (lit. “in favor of”) is an archaic, Latin-derived phrase meaning “for the sake of/for the benefit of.” Del mondo = “of the world.” Che mal vive is a relative clause: che = “which/that,” mal is an adverb “badly/evilly,” and vive is present indicative “lives” (characteristic present: the world that lives poorly/immorally).
segments.purgatorio-32-g90: Al = a + il, “to/on the”; carro is the noun “chariot” (here the triumphal vehicle). Tieni is the 2nd person singular imperative (from tenere) meaning “keep” or “hold”; or it can be read as present with hortatory force, but context is an instruction. Or is an adverb meaning “now”; li occhi = “the eyes” (direct object) — possessive is omitted (poetic: “keep your eyes” is implied). Word order (prepositional phrase before verb) is natural in poetry.
segments.purgatorio-32-g91: E is “and.” Quel che = “that which/what” (demonstrative + relative pronoun). Vedi is present indicative 2nd person singular “you see” (referring to what he is about to witness); together: “and that which you see.”
segments.purgatorio-32-g92: Ritornato is a past participle used adverbially/temporal-statally: “having returned” or “upon your return.” Di là = “from there” (di + demonstrative/locative là). This clause sets the time when the following command should be carried out.
segments.purgatorio-32-g93: Fa (from fare) here is an imperative/jussive formula meaning “make (it so)/see to it.” Che introduces a subordinate clause (“that”); tu is the subject “you.” Scrive is the older/poetic present form used as a jussive/subjunctive (“write”/“that you write”) — in modern Italian one would expect scriva, but Dante often uses scrive in this construction. The whole means “see to it that you write.”
segments.purgatorio-32-g94: Così is an adverb meaning 'thus' or 'so' and stands alone with the name Beatrice in an emphatic, detached phrase (typical of Dante's narrative transitions).
segments.purgatorio-32-g95: Simple coordinate particle e 'and' followed by the subject pronoun io 'I' set off by a comma for emphasis; io is the nominative pronoun stressing the speaker.
segments.purgatorio-32-g96: A relative clause introduced by che 'who' (referring back to 'I'): tutto ai piedi d’i suoi comandamenti era divoto is a predicative structure with era (imperfect/past) 'was' and divoto used adjectivally 'devoted' or 'wholly devoted.' Ai piedi 'at the feet (of)' is idiomatic, and tutto here has the sense 'completely/entirely.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g97: La mente e li occhi is the direct object (my mind and my eyes). Ov' is a contracted form of ove/ove 'where,' and ella volle is passato remoto 'she willed/desired'; diedi is passato remoto 'I gave' (dare) and takes la mente e li occhi as its object: 'I gave my mind and eyes where she willed.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g98: Non...mai is a negative pair meaning 'never.' Scese is passato remoto of scendere 'descended' and the verb precedes its subject foco for poetic inversion. Con sì veloce moto is an adverbial phrase 'with so swift motion.' Di spessa nube is a genitive/of-phrase 'from a thick cloud' (de + noun in older diction).
segments.purgatorio-32-g99: Quando introduces a temporal clause 'when'; piove is the impersonal present of piovere 'it rains/falls' describing the kind of descent; da quel confine 'from that boundary' uses da as 'from' and the relative clause che più va remoto 'which goes/lies most remote' uses present indicative va to mean 'lies/is situated.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g100: Com' is an aphetic form of come 'as/just as'; vidi is passato remoto 'I saw' and takes the infinitive calar as its object complement (vidi calar = 'I saw...swoop/descend'). L'uccel di Giove 'the bird of Jove' is the thing seen; per l'alber giù is a motion phrase 'down through the tree' with per indicating movement through and giù the adverb 'down.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g101: Rompendo is a gerund 'breaking' that describes a simultaneous action (the bird swooping while breaking). De la is an archaic form of della 'of/from the,' and scorza means 'bark' (the tree's outer layer) — so 'breaking through the bark.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g102: Non che here functions idiomatically to add the further items affected — roughly 'and even' or 'not sparing' — linking what follows to rompendo: d’i fiori e de le foglie nove specifies 'the flowers and the new leaves' also broken/offended by the action; de is again the archaic of/of the.
segments.purgatorio-32-g103: E ferì is another main clause: ferì is passato remoto (he/it struck) from ferire and takes ’l carro as direct object ('struck the chariot'). Di tutta sua forza is an ablative/expressive phrase 'with all its force,' modifying the verb.
segments.purgatorio-32-g104: Ond' is a contracted form of onde/ond (introducing a result or cause: 'whereupon'/'thus'), el is an archaic form of the pronoun/article 'it/he' (here 'it' = the chariot), and piegò is passato remoto 'bent.' Come nave in fortuna is a simile 'like a ship in a storm' (fortuna here meaning 'storm' in a nautical sense).
segments.purgatorio-32-g105: Vinta is the past participle 'overcome/defeated' agreeing with the subject (implicit it/the chariot), da l'onda is an agent/means phrase 'by the wave' (da = by/from). The participle phrase explains the manner of the bending.
segments.purgatorio-32-g106: Or is the adverb 'now' used repeatedly for rhythmic effect; da introduces the source/cause and poggia here refers to a nautical motion or heel (literally 'a leaning'), so 'now by a swell/leaning.' The short phrase is elliptical — 'now [overcome] by a poggia.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g107: Again da introduces the cause and orza is a nautical term meaning 'to head up/to luff toward the wind' — 'now by a luff.' The pair or da poggia, or da orza contrasts leeward and windward actions; Dante uses terse nautical vocabulary to describe the ship's pitching.
segments.purgatorio-32-g10: Ma poi ch' + clause is a temporal connector: 'But after/when'. 'al poco' is an idiom meaning 'a little' or 'a short time'; the article + noun functions adverbially. 'il viso' is the grammatical subject (literally 'the face' but used here for sight/vision). 'riformossi' is an archaic/poetic past-form (reflexive) meaning 'was/ became restored' or 're-formed' (third-person singular perfect). The parenthetical 'e dico...mi rimossi' is a comment clarifying 'al poco'.
segments.purgatorio-32-g11: 'vidi' is simple past (I saw). The sequence '’n sul braccio destro esser rivolto lo glorïoso essercito' is an object clause introduced by 'vidi': literally 'I saw the glorious host to be turned on the right arm' — '’n' is an elided/informal form appearing before 'sul' (poetic contraction), 'sul' = 'on the', 'esser rivolto' uses the infinitive 'essere' + participle 'rivolto' to report a state observed ('to be turned/toward'). Word order (infinitive + participle after locative) is typical of Dante's descriptive style.
segments.purgatorio-32-g12: 'e tornarsi' continues the report seen by the narrator: the reflexive infinitive 'tornarsi' (to turn oneself) is used in coordination with the preceding infinitive construction, describing motion observed. 'col' is the contraction of 'con il' (with the), and the parallel 'e con le sette fiamme' repeats 'with' to list accompanying elements; 'al volto' ('to the face') indicates the direction towards which they turned. The overall clause describes the host's turning accompanied by the sun and seven flames.
segments.purgatorio-32-g13: 'Come' is a comparative/simile 'as'. 'sotto li scudi' means 'under the shields' (prepositional phrase). 'per salvarsi' is an infinitival purpose phrase 'to save themselves / to take refuge'. 'volgesi' (from volgere/volgersi) is a present-form used here as 'turns' (third-person singular) with 'schiera' (troop) as subject; the verb precedes the subject in Dante's word order.
segments.purgatorio-32-g14: This short clause continues the image: 'e' = 'and'. 'sé gira' is reflexive 'turns itself' (gira = turns; 'sé' = itself). 'col segno' ('with the sign/standard') specifies how they turn (i.e., guided by a signal/standard). The reflexive shows the troop's motion about its own axis.
segments.purgatorio-32-g15: 'prima che' introduces a temporal subordinate clause and takes the subjunctive in Dante. 'possa' is the present subjunctive of 'potere' ('may/can'), here 'can' in a potential sense. 'tutta in sé mutarsi' is an infinitival phrase 'to change entirely in itself' (mutarsi = change/be transformed), so the clause means 'before it can wholly change itself.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g16: 'quella milizia del celeste regno' is the subject phrase 'that host/militia of the heavenly kingdom'. 'che procedeva' is a relative clause: 'which was proceeding/advancing'; 'procedeva' is the imperfect (past continuous) indicating ongoing movement in the past narrative.
segments.purgatorio-32-g17: 'tutta' is emphatic ('entirely/whole'), modifying the verb. 'trapassonne' is an archaic past-tense form meaning roughly 'passed through' or 'pierced through' (third-person plural past), describing the host's movement. 'pria che' = 'before' and the subordinate uses the imperfect subjunctive 'piegasse' (from piegare) 'bent' because Dante often uses the subjunctive after temporal conjunctions to mark anteriority or potentiality. The phrase 'il carro il primo legno' places the object ('the chariot's first beam') after the verb in poetic order.
segments.purgatorio-32-g18: 'Indi' = 'then/thence'. 'a le rote' = 'to the wheels' (here 'rote' can mean celestial wheels/spheres); 'si tornar le donne' is inverted: the verb phrase 'si tornar' (to turn themselves) precedes the subject 'le donne' (the ladies), a common poetic inversion in Dante. The reflexive 'si' marks the action as performed upon themselves.
segments.purgatorio-32-g19: This clause links the action of the ladies to the motion of the griffin: 'e' = 'and'. '’l grifon' = 'the griffin' (poetic form). 'mosse' here is the past form of 'muovere' meaning 'moved' (Dante uses simple past forms freely). 'il benedetto carco' = 'the blessed/load/burden' (carried thing). 'sì' = 'so' (introducing the resulting condition).
segments.purgatorio-32-g20: 'che' here introduces a result or explanatory clause 'so that'. 'però' is an adverb 'therefore/yet/so' used to emphasize the result. 'nulla penna' = 'not a single feather' (lit. 'no feather'), with 'nulla' negating. 'crollonne' is an archaic/dialectal past/impf form (third-person plural) of 'crollare' meaning 'to tremble/shake' rather than 'to fall' here: the sense is that not one feather was disturbed.
segments.purgatorio-32-g21: This is a complex sentence with a relative clause followed by a coordinated subject and verb. 'La bella donna che mi trasse al varco' = 'the beautiful lady who drew me to the passage' ('trasse' is past historic of 'trarre' = 'drew/pulled/rescued'). Then 'e Stazio e io seguitavam la rota' = 'and Statius and I were following the wheel' ('seguitavam' is an archaic imperfect first-person plural = 'we followed/kept following'). Finally the relative 'che fé l'orbita sua con minore arco' qualifies 'la rota': 'which made/described its orbit with a smaller arc' ('fé' = fece, past historic 'made').
segments.purgatorio-32-g22: Sì is an adverb meaning “so/thus” used as a manner marker; passeggiando is a gerund (present participle) forming an adverbial clause “while/as (we) were walking.” The noun phrase l’alta selva vòta follows: the article + adjective + noun + an archaic spelling vòta (modern vuota) meaning “empty.” Together the phrase describes the setting in which the action of the next clauses takes place.
segments.purgatorio-32-g23: colpa di introduces a causal phrase “by/on account of the fault/guilt of …”; quella is a feminine demonstrative (= “that one,” here referring to the woman previously mentioned). ch’ is the contraction of che (relative pronoun “who/which”), and al serpente crese is a relative clause with crese (past historic of crescere, used poetically) meaning “arose/was brought into being/grew.” The whole clause means “because of the fault of that one who brought forth/caused the serpent (to be).”
segments.purgatorio-32-g24: Temprava is an imperfect tense (third-person singular) of temprar(e) used here to mean “tempered/softened/regulated” and takes i passi as its direct object; word order places the verb before its subject/predicate nominative. Un’angelica nota is the agent/means expressed after the verb (an angelic sound/note moderated their pace). Nota here is used in the sense of a musical or audible note (poetic usage).
segments.purgatorio-32-g25: Forse is an adverb (“perhaps”) introducing a conjecture. In tre voli is an adverbial measure (“in/within three flights”), tanto spazio is the direct object (“so much space/distance”), and prese is passato remoto (third-person singular of prendere) meaning “took” or “covered.” Disfrenata saetta is a noun phrase (an unbridled/wild bolt) that functions as the subject; the verb–subject order is a poetic inversion.
segments.purgatorio-32-g26: quanto here introduces a comparative clause meaning “as much as.” Eramo is the imperfect of essere (“we were”), and rimossi is a past participle used adjectivally/passively (“removed/moved away” or “separated”); together the phrase means “as far as we had been moved/removed.”
segments.purgatorio-32-g27: quando introduces a temporal subordinate clause (“when”). Bëatrice is the proper name; scese is passato remoto (third-person singular of scendere) meaning “descended.” The clause gives the time-reference for the preceding comparison.
segments.purgatorio-32-g28: Io is the emphatic subject “I.” senti’ is a contracted/poetic spelling of sentii (passato remoto of sentire) meaning “I heard”; it is followed by the infinitive mormorare, a classic construction after verbs of perception meaning “I heard (them) murmur.” a tutti marks the people producing the murmur (dative/agentive use), and the quoted word «Adamo» is the content of that murmur.
segments.purgatorio-32-g29: poi is a temporal adverb “then.” cerchiaro is an archaic/poetic past form (here functioning as a third-person plural past) meaning “they searched/sought” (variant orthography of cercarono); it takes una pianta dispogliata as its direct object (“a tree stripped of …”). dispogliata di foglie e d’altra fronda is a participial adjective phrase “stripped of leaves and of other foliage,” and in ciascun ramo specifies location “on each branch.”
segments.purgatorio-32-g30: La coma sua is a noun phrase meaning “her hair/lock” (coma in older Italian = a lock/tress of hair). The definite article + noun + possessive is a standard, emphatic subject phrase.
segments.purgatorio-32-g31: che is a relative pronoun “which.” tanto… quanto is a correlative comparative structure; here che tanto si dilata più quanto più è sù literally yields “which so much expands itself, the more the more it is up” — idiomatically “which spreads out more the higher it is.” si dilata is a reflexive/intransitive present-form verb (“expands/extends itself”), and the repeated più…quanto più is the poetic comparative “the more… the more.”
segments.purgatorio-32-g32: fora here is an archaic verbal form used to express a conditional/irrealis sense (in context: “would have been” / “would be seen”); da l’Indi means “by/from the Indians” (agents or observers), ne’ boschi lor = “in their woods/forests” (ne’ = in + article), and per altezza ammirata gives the reason or quality “admired for its height.” The whole clause says that, from the Indians’ point of view in their woods, it would have been admired for its height. Note: several words use archaic spellings and fora is not the modern verb for “to pierce” here but a literary/archaic auxiliary sense.
segments.purgatorio-32-g77: Vedi is a second-person singular imperative (or a vivid present 'you see') meaning 'look/see'. 'la compagnia' is the direct object (the group surrounding the lady). The relative clause 'che la circonda' ('that surrounds her') uses che as a relative pronoun and la as the feminine object pronoun referring back to the lady.
segments.purgatorio-32-g78: ‘Li altri’ = 'the others' (archaic li = modern i). 'dopo ’l grifon' = 'after the griffin' (dopo + definite article + griffin). 'sen vanno' is the enclitic/separable form of 'se ne vanno' meaning 'they go away' or 'they go off/along (after it)'; 'suso' is an adverb meaning 'up' (upward). The final prepositional phrase 'con più dolce canzone e più profonda' modifies 'vanno', describing the manner: 'with a sweeter and more profound song.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g79: E se introduces a conditional/hypothetical clause ('And if/whether'), here meaning 'and if it was more extended.' 'più' = 'more'; 'fu' is the perfect/past of essere ('was'). 'lo suo parlar' = 'her/its speech' with the article lo + possessive suo (poetic word order: article + possessor + noun). 'diffuso' is a past participle/adjective meaning 'diffuse/extended' describing 'parlar'.
segments.purgatorio-32-g80: A short, colloquial clause: 'non so' = 'I do not know'. 'non' negates the verb 'so' (1st person singular present of sapere).
segments.purgatorio-32-g81: 'però che' here introduces the reason/explanation ('because' / 'for'), followed by the explanatory content. 'già' = 'already'. 'ne li occhi' is an archaic locative expression meaning 'in (my) eyes' ('ne' + plural definite article li + occhi); 'm’era' = 'there was to me' (m’ = me; era = was). 'quella' = 'that one' (a person/thing present in his sight). The relative clause 'ch’ad altro intender m’avea chiuso' uses ch’ = 'which/that'; 'ad altro intender' = 'to understand other things' (ad + infinitive used with intender = 'to understand'); 'm’avea' = 'had to me' (i.e., 'had closed me') and 'chiuso' = 'closed' (past participle), so the clause means 'which had closed me to understanding other things.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g82: Sola ('alone') modifies the understood feminine subject (Beatrice) and emphasizes solitude. Sedeasi is an archaic/poetic inflected form of sedere with enclitic -si (reflexive/archaic marker); here it functions as the finite verb 'sits' (Dante's usage often employs such forms instead of the modern siede). 'In su la terra vera' ('upon the true earth') uses the prepositional combination in + su to mean 'on/upon' and places 'terra vera' (the true/real ground) after the preposition as its object.
segments.purgatorio-32-g83: 'Come' introduces a simile 'as/like'; 'guardia' ('guard') is the noun being compared. 'Lasciata' is a past participle meaning 'left' and agrees with guardia (so 'left there'); 'lì' is 'there'. 'Del plaustro' = 'of the wagon' (del = di + il). The relative clause 'che legar vidi a la biforme fera' literally is 'which I saw bound to the two-formed beast': che = 'which/that' (introducing the clause), legar is an infinitive used after vidi to convey a seen action ('to bind' used here with force of 'bound'), and vidi is the first-person past 'I saw'; 'a la biforme fera' indicates the entity 'to the two-formed beast' to which the wagon was bound. Overall the clause means 'like a guard left there of the wagon which I saw bound to the two-formed beast.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g84: Poetic word order: the verb facevan (they made) appears before its object claustro and the agent (le sette ninfe) follows; di sé attaches to claustro and means 'of themselves' (i.e. 'for themselves'). 'In cerchio' is an adverbial phrase placed first for emphasis: 'in a circle.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g85: Con introduces a manner phrase 'with those lamps in hand'; quei lumi is 'those lamps' (physical lights) and in mano is a fixed phrase 'in (their) hand.' The relative che son sicuri... modifies lumi: son is the present indicative 'are' and sicuri d'Aquilone e d'Austro means 'secure/safe from the north and south winds.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g33: Beato is an adjective meaning “blessed.” Se’ is an archaic contracted form of sei (you are) with an apostrophe showing the elision; together the phrase is a direct address meaning “blessed are you.”
segments.purgatorio-32-g34: Grifon is a vocative — the speaker is addressing the griffin directly. No verb is expressed here; the name stands alone as the addressee.
segments.purgatorio-32-g35: Che introduces a relative clause modifying “griffin” (here = “who”). Non discindi is present-tense, 2nd-person singular of discindere/discindare (here ‘do not tear apart’). Col is the contraction of con + il (“with the”), becco is the instrument “beak,” d’esto = di + esto (“of this/this here”), legno = “wood.” Dolce al gusto is a descriptive phrase “sweet to the taste” (dolce modifying legno, with al gusto = ‘to the taste’).
segments.purgatorio-32-g36: Poscia che is a causal/consecutive conjunction here meaning “since”/“because.” Mal is an adverb “badly,” si is the reflexive particle, and torce (third-person singular) means “twists” (the belly twists). Il ventre is the subject “the belly,” and quindi = “from that / thereby.” The clause gives the reason or consequence for not tearing the wood.
segments.purgatorio-32-g37: Così = “thus/so.” Dintorno a = a prepositional phrase “around” + a. L’albero robusto = “the sturdy tree.” Gridaron is passato remoto (or poetic past) third-person plural of gridare, “they cried/shouted”; li altri is the subject “the others” placed after the verb (inverted order common in poetry).
segments.purgatorio-32-g38: E = “and.” L’animal = “the creature/animal.” Binato is an adjective meaning “paired / twofold,” here describing the animal as two-bodied or two-natured (a feature of a griffin). The phrase is elliptical: the twofold animal also utters what follows.
segments.purgatorio-32-g39: Sì here = “so/thus” (an adverb introducing the clause). Si conserva is a reflexive/passive impersonal construction meaning “is preserved / is kept” (third-person singular). Il seme d’ogne giusto = “the seed/offspring of every righteous [man]”; ogne is the archaic form of ogni. The whole is a declarative sentence in quotation marks.
segments.purgatorio-32-g40: E = “and.” Vòlto is a past participle (from volgere) used verbally: “having turned” or “turned.” Al introduces the following noun (al + temo). Ch’ elli avea tirato is a relative clause: ch’ = che “that/which,” elli = “he,” avea tirato = pluperfect ‘had pulled’ (he had pulled). The precise meaning of temo in this line is uncertain from the lemma given; syntactically it is the object/goal of the turning.
segments.purgatorio-32-g41: Trasselo is a fused archaic form (trasse + lo) meaning “he drew/dragged it” (third-person past) with the clitic object -lo. Al piè = “to the foot/base,” de la vedova frasca = “of the bereft/widowed bough” (vedova figuratively ‘bereft’ or ‘stripped,’ frasca = ‘bough/twig’). The clause says he dragged it to the base of that bough.
segments.purgatorio-32-g42: E = “and.” Quel di lei = “that (one) of hers” (quel as demonstrative + di lei ‘belonging to her’). A lei = dative “to her.” Lasciò is passato remoto (he left / he let), and legato is a past participle used adjectively “tied/bound.” The line says he left her own (something) tied to her.
segments.purgatorio-32-g43: Come = “like/as.” Le nostre piante = “our plants.” This is the simile opening the comparison between the plants and whatever was described before.
segments.purgatorio-32-g44: Quando = “when.” Casca giù is a poetic expression meaning “falls down” (casca = falls), la gran luce mischiata con quella che raggia = “the great light, mixed with that which radiates/gleams,” dietro a la celeste lasca = “behind the celestial lasca” (lasca is an archaic adjective/noun; here it pictures a loose place/opening of the heavens). The whole subordinate clause situates the moment when the plants react.
segments.purgatorio-32-g45: Turgide = “swollen/distended.” Fansi is an archaic/poetic third-person plural form (from farsi or fare) meaning “they became / they were made” — an older verbal form used here instead of modern furono or si fecero.
segments.purgatorio-32-g46: E poi = “and then.” Si rinovella is present (or gnomic) reflexive: “each renews itself” or “is renewed.” Di suo color = “in its own color,” ciascuna = “each one” (referring back to the plants).
segments.purgatorio-32-g47: Pria che = “before.” ’L sole giunga is a subordinate clause introduced by pria che and requiring the subjunctive in Italian; giunga is therefore the present subjunctive of giungere (“the sun come/arrive”). Li suoi corsier = “his chargers/steeds” (a poetic image of the sun’s horses), sotto altra stella = “under another star.”
segments.purgatorio-32-g48: Men che … e più che = comparative phrasing: “less than … and more than …” (men che = less than, più che = more than). Di rose … di vïole = “than roses … than violets.” Colore aprendo is a participial phrase “opening/showing color,” meaning the plants show a color less like roses and more like violets.
segments.purgatorio-32-g49: S’innovò is an older form of si innovò (third-person singular past) meaning “it renewed itself” or “was renewed.” La pianta = “the plant.” The clause states the plant was renewed in this way.
segments.purgatorio-32-g50: Che = relative pronoun “which.” Prima avea = imperfect ‘had before’ (it previously had). Le ramora = “the boughs/branches” (ramora = branches), sì sole = “so sun-exposed” (sì = so; sole invoked as ‘sun’ or ‘sunlight’ describing exposure). The relative clause describes the plant’s earlier condition.
segments.purgatorio-32-g65: 'Quali' introduces a simile: 'like/as when.' 'a veder' is an infinitival phrase meaning 'at the sight of' (literally 'to see'); 'de' fioretti del melo' = 'of the little blossoms of the apple-tree' (de' = dei). The relative clause 'che del suo pome li angeli fa ghiotti e perpetüe nozze fa nel cielo' uses che + clause where 'fa' (3rd sing.) governs the adjective 'ghiotti' ('makes (them) greedy') and again 'fa' 'makes' with the plural noun 'nozze' (perpetual nuptials) — poetic word order places objects and complements after the verb.
segments.purgatorio-32-g66: A simple nominative list of proper names 'Pietro e Giovanni e Iacopo' followed by two past participles 'condotti e vinti' used adjectivally; participles describe their state (having been led and overcome). The comma signals continuation of the descriptive clause.
segments.purgatorio-32-g67: 'ritornaro' is an archaic/historical past (poetic form of ritornarono) meaning 'they returned' or 'they turned back.' 'a la parola' = 'to the word' (the point/utterance), and the relative phrase 'da la qual furon maggior sonni rotti' literally 'from which greater sleeps were broken' uses 'furon' (archaic past of 'furono') + participle 'rotti' to form a passive meaning 'their deeper sleep was broken' (i.e., they were more fully awakened).
segments.purgatorio-32-g68: 'e videro' = 'and they saw.' 'scemata' is a past participle used adjectivally = 'diminished'; 'loro scuola' = 'their school' (school as a group or followers). The correlative 'così ... come ...' links two examples: 'both that of Moses and of Elijah.' Word order mirrors Latin/poetic inversion.
segments.purgatorio-32-g69: Inverted phrasing: 'e al maestro suo cangiata stola' literally 'and to their/master his master a changed stole' — i.e. 'and their master's stole was changed.' 'cangiata' is a past participle modifying 'stola'; 'al maestro suo' is an indirect-possessive construction where 'suo' agrees with 'maestro.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g70: 'tal' = 'so/thus' and 'torna’' is a contracted poetic past first-person ('tornai'/'I returned'); placing 'io' after the verb gives emphasis: 'thus I returned.' The apostrophe marks elision common in Dante's verse.
segments.purgatorio-32-g71: 'e vidi' = 'and I saw;' 'quella pia' = 'that pious/holy woman.' 'sovra me starsi' uses the reflexive verb 'starsi' meaning 'to be situated/stand' with 'sovra me' = 'above me.' The relative clause 'che conducitrice fu de’ miei passi lungo ’l fiume pria' uses 'fu' (past 'was') to mean 'who had been the guide of my steps formerly along the river' — 'de’ miei passi' = 'of my steps' (i.e., who guided my walks).
segments.purgatorio-32-g72: 'E' = 'and.' 'tutto in dubbio' is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'completely/entirely in doubt' (here 'tutto' functions adverbially), and 'dissi' is the first-person simple past 'I said.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g73: Short direct question. 'Ov’' is a contracted 'ov'è' (where is), with the apostrophe before a vowel; 'è' is the present of 'essere' ('is'), followed by the proper name 'Beatrice.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g74: This segment contains only punctuation (a single period) and carries no lexical or grammatical material; it functions as a pause or line break in the text.
segments.purgatorio-32-g75: Ond' is a contracted/disjunctive form meaning 'thereupon' or 'whence' used as a discourse connector ('thereupon'/'then'); ella is the third-person feminine pronoun 'she' and here marks the speaker ('thereupon she:').
segments.purgatorio-32-g76: Vedi is an imperative (or vocative/directional present) meaning 'look/see'; lei is the accusative feminine pronoun 'her' (object of vedi). The phrase 'sotto la fronda nova' is a prepositional phrase 'under/beneath the fresh foliage' (fronda = leafy bough/foliage, nova = 'new/fresh'). Sedere is an infinitive complement after vedi meaning 'to sit' or more naturally 'sitting' ('see her sitting'); the sequence 'in su' (in + su) means 'upon' or 'on' and governs 'la sua radice' = 'her root' (sua = her/its). Word order places the imperative, object, and location before the infinitive participial idea 'sedere' to yield 'See her sitting beneath the fresh foliage upon her root.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g108: Poscia (adverb) opens the clause: 'then/afterwards'. Vidi is the 1st‑person singular passato remoto of vedere ('I saw') and governs the following infinitive avventarsi — a common perception construction (I saw [someone] do something). Ne la cuna del trïunfal veiculo is a locative phrase where ne = 'in/into' and cuna 'cradle' takes the genitive del trïunfal veiculo; the subject of the infinitive, una volpe, follows (typical Italian inversion for poetic emphasis). The relative clause che d’ogne pasto buon parea digiuna uses parea (imperf./poetic imperfect of parere, 'seemed') + the adjective digiuna ('fasting/hungry') to describe the fox.
segments.purgatorio-32-g109: Ma is the adversative conjunction 'but', introducing the contrastive clause that follows.
segments.purgatorio-32-g110: Riprendendo is a present gerund/participle of riprendere used here in the sense 'rebuking' (not 'resuming'), giving a simultaneous or attendant action. Lei is the object pronoun 'her' (referring back to the fox). Di laide colpe is an object/agent phrase meaning 'for loathsome faults/sins' (di marks the fault or cause).
segments.purgatorio-32-g111: La donna mia ('my lady') is the subject (possessive after the noun is normal in older/poetic Italian). The clitic la before volse is the object pronoun 'her' (the fox). Volse is passato remoto of volgere, here 'turned' or 'drove' (caused to go). In tanta futa 'in such a flight', futa is an archaic noun 'flight/retreat'. Quanto introduces a comparison 'as much as' and sofferser is an archaic passato remoto (3rd pl.) 'they endured' whose subject is l'ossa ('the bones'); sanza polpe = 'without flesh'.
segments.purgatorio-32-g112: Poscia again 'then/afterwards'. Per indi 'by/through that place' is an adverbial phrase; ond’ (contraction of 'onde') introduces a relative clause 'from which'. Era pri(a) venuta is a compound past (auxiliary essere + past participle) meaning 'had come before' (pluperfect sense), with the participle venuta agreeing in gender with the feminine subject implied.
segments.purgatorio-32-g113: L’aguglia ('the eagle') is the logical subject; vidi scender is the same perception construction as before ('I saw [it] descend'), where scender is the infinitive. Giù ne l’arca del carro specifies direction/place: 'down into the ark of the chariot' (ne/l'arca = 'in the ark'). E lasciar lei di sé pennuta continues with another infinitive lasciar ('leave') governed by vidi — 'and leave her feathered by/of itself' (di sé = 'from itself', pennuta = 'feathered').
segments.purgatorio-32-g114: E qual esce di cuor che si rammarca is a simile: qual = 'as/like one who', esce (present/past‑narrative) 'issues/goes forth', di cuor 'from the heart', che si rammarca is a relative clause with si (reflexive/middle) and rammarca ('laments/is grieved'), describing the tone of the voice.
segments.purgatorio-32-g115: Tal voce uscì del cielo: tal voce 'such a voice' + uscì (passato remoto of uscire) 'came out' 'del cielo' = 'from heaven'. E cotal disse: cotal (archaic adverb) = 'thus/so', and disse is passato remoto 'said' — the voice utters the following speech.
segments.purgatorio-32-g116: O is the vocative/exclamatory 'O/Oh'. Navicella mia is a vocative 'my little vessel/ship' (navicella is a diminutive; possessive after the noun is normal in poetic Italian).
segments.purgatorio-32-g117: Com’ is a contraction of come 'how'; mal is the adverb 'badly/ill' and se’ is an archaic form of sei ('you are') used here as the verb 'are' in the 2nd person singular. Carca is a predicate adjective agreeing with navicella (feminine): 'how ill‑laden you are!' — an exclamatory reproach addressing the vessel.
segments.purgatorio-32-g118: This segment is a punctuation mark (a period) indicating the end of the sentence; it contains no lexical material and carries no grammatical features. It does not correspond to any Italian word and therefore has no syntactic or semantic role in the verse. Treat it simply as the sentence terminator in the edition you provided.
segments.purgatorio-32-g119: "Poi parve a me" is a common narrative phrase meaning "then it seemed to me," with parve (past tense of parere) taking the indirect object introduced by the preposition a + pronoun me. The conjunction che introduces a subordinate clause with the subjunctive aprisse (s’aprisse: si + aprisse) — the reflexive clitic s’ (si) is used here to form an intransitive/reflexive sense 'opened' — and tr’ (contraction of tra) + ambo le ruote means "between both the wheels."
segments.purgatorio-32-g120: VidI is the simple past "I saw," taking the infinitive construction uscirne (uscire + enclitic ne = "come out of it") as its object; un drago is the noun phrase subject of the following relative clause. In the relative clause che ... fisse, the verb fisse is a past-tense form meaning "fixed," and the sequence per lo carro sù la coda places the adverbial phrase "up along the chariot" (per lo carro sù) before the direct object la coda.
segments.purgatorio-32-g121: The clause begins a simile with come "as/like." Vespa is the concrete noun "wasp," and che introduces a relative clause whose verb ritragge is present (narrative present) meaning "draws back/withdraws." L’ago is the direct object "the needle" (here functioning as its sting).
segments.purgatorio-32-g122: A participial phrase: a sé traendo = "drawing toward itself" where a sé is a reflexive prepositional phrase meaning "to itself" and traendo is the present participle/gerund of trarre "drawing." La coda maligna is the noun phrase "the wicked/evil tail," with maligna as an adjective modifying coda.
segments.purgatorio-32-g123: Trasse is the remote past (passato remoto) of trarre, "it pulled/drew," and del fondo is a prepositional phrase (di + il) meaning "from the bottom/depths"—here indicating source: it pulled the tail up out of the depths.
segments.purgatorio-32-g124: Gissen is an archaic/poetic past form meaning "lay/was lying" (from giacere); the doubled adjective vago vago is a repeated descriptor meaning "wandering/roving" (reduplication for emphasis), describing the dragon's state after emerging.
segments.purgatorio-32-g143: Ma is an adversative particle 'but'. Perché here introduces the circumstance ('because/when' — an older usage meaning roughly 'when' or 'since') and governs the clause that follows. L’ is the elided definite article il + vowel, modifying occhio; occhio is used metaphorically as 'gaze/eye'. Cupido is an adjective meaning 'desirous' (from cupido = desire) and vagante is a present participle/adjective 'wandering' placed after the noun (poetic word order). A me are the prepositional complement 'toward me', and rivolse is passato remoto, 3rd person singular of rivolgere — 'turned (toward)'.
segments.purgatorio-32-g144: Quel is the demonstrative 'that' pointing back to a previously seen figure; feroce is an adjective 'fierce/cruel' modifying drudo (an archaic noun meaning 'paramour/lover'). La is the feminine direct-object pronoun 'her' (referring to the woman). Flagellò is passato remoto, 3rd singular of flagellare 'whipped/scourged'. Dal is the contraction da + il 'from the', capo 'head', infin 'until/as far as' (poetic shortened infinit), le piante = 'the soles' (piante here = soles of the feet), so the phrase means 'scourged her from head to soles'.
segments.purgatorio-32-g145: Poi is an adverb 'then/afterwards', a simple connective advancing the narrative.
segments.purgatorio-32-g146: Di sospetto pieno is a prepositional phrase 'full of suspicion' (di + noun + adjective), with sospetto 'suspicion' and pieno 'full' placed after it in idiomatic order. E d’ira crudo parallels the first phrase: d’ira = 'of/with anger' and crudo is an adjective 'cruel/harsh' modifying the whole phrase; the comma keeps this as a characterizing description of the subject that follows.
segments.purgatorio-32-g147: Disciolse is passato remoto, 3rd person singular of disciogliere 'loosen/release', used here to mean 'set free' or 'let go' (it refers to a held creature). Il is the masculine singular definite article 'the' and mostro = 'monster' (the creature just released). The phrase is compact: 'he loosed the monster.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g148: E = 'and'. Trassel is the past verb trasse ('he pulled/dragged') plus the enclitic object pronoun -l (l' = lo/it) attached — a poetic contraction meaning 'and he dragged it'. Per la selva is a simple prepositional phrase 'through the wood/forest' indicating the path.
segments.purgatorio-32-g149: Tanto che = 'so that' introducing a result clause. Sol is an archaic/poetic form of 'solo' meaning 'only' (here 'only/she alone'); di lei = 'by her' or 'from her' (agency/source). Mi fece is composed of the indirect object clitic mi ('me') plus fece, passato remoto of fare 'made/caused', so 'she made me' or 'she served me as'. Scudo is a noun 'shield/protection' and occurs as the complement of fece (fece scudo = 'made a shield'). A la puttana e a la nova belva: a here means 'against/toward' in the sense of protection 'against the prostitute and the new beast'; la puttana = 'the prostitute' and la nova belva = 'the new beast' (nova = new).
segments.purgatorio-32-g125: Quel che rimase is a short relative clause: 'Quel' ('that') + relative 'che' ('which/that') + passato remoto 'rimase' (rimanere) 'remained/was left'. In English it works as 'that which remained' or simply 'what remained.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g126: Poetic word order: 'come' = 'as/like'; 'da' introduces origin or cause ('from/by'); 'gramigna vivace' is an adjective + noun ('lively couch-grass'); 'terra' is the head noun 'earth/ground' placed at the end for effect. Together: 'like ground lively with couch-grass.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g127: 'da la' here is the older, uncontracted form of 'dalla' ('from/by the'); 'piuma' is the feminine noun 'feather.' Grammatically this is an instrumental/origin phrase: 'by the feather' or 'from the feather.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g128: 'offerta' is a past participle (agreeing with 'piuma') meaning 'offered'; 'forse' is the adverb 'perhaps'; the prepositional phrase 'con intenzion sana e benigna' qualifies the offering (intention = purpose), with two adjectives 'sana' (sound/wholesome) and 'benigna' (benign/kind).
segments.purgatorio-32-g129: 'si ricoperse' is a reflexive / middle-voice passato remoto of ricoprire: 'si' marks the reflexive/middle meaning 'was covered / covered itself again'; 'ricoperse' = 'covered over/again.' The construction indicates the thing became covered anew.
segments.purgatorio-32-g130: This line uses an archaic fused particle 'funne' (here to be read as fu + ne, i.e. 'was of it/thereby was') + past participle 'ricoperta' meaning 'covered'; the phrase then lists the affected parts 'e l’una e l’altra rota e ’l temo' ('both one and the other wheel and the axle'). In short: 'and both wheels and the axle were covered by it.' Note: 'funne' is an archaic fused form and 'temo' is a technical noun referring to the vehicle's shaft/axle.
segments.purgatorio-32-g131: 'in tanto che' = 'to such an extent that'; 'più' here has the sense 'any longer/no longer' or 'more'; 'tiene' (tenere) 'holds/keeps'; 'un sospir' 'a sigh' is the object; 'la bocca aperta' 'the mouth open' is a descriptive phrase. The whole clause: 'so much that the mouth can hold a sigh no longer' (i.e. a sigh is forced out).
segments.purgatorio-32-g132: 'Trasformato così' is a past participle phrase 'thus transformed' modifying the subject 'l dificio santo' ('the holy edifice'). 'mise' is the passato remoto of mettere ('placed/put'); 'fuor' = 'fuori' 'out'; 'teste' = 'heads'; 'per le parti sue' = 'on/through its parts' (i.e. on its sides).
segments.purgatorio-32-g133: 'tre' = 'three'; 'sovra' = 'above'; '’l temo' = 'the axle/shaft' (temo is a technical/archaic noun); 'e una in ciascun canto' = 'and one in each corner/quarter.' Word order places numerals and locations compactly for rhythm.
segments.purgatorio-32-g134: 'Le prime' = 'the first ones' (feminine plural); 'eran' = imperfetto/archaic form of 'erano' 'were'; 'cornute' = 'horned' (adjective); 'come bue' = 'like an ox.' The adjective follows the noun in typical poetic order.
segments.purgatorio-32-g135: 'ma' = 'but'; 'le quattro' = 'the four (ones)'; 'un sol corno' = 'one single horn' (sol = solo, 'only/sole'); 'avean' = imperfect 'they had'; 'per fronte' = 'on the forehead' (per used to indicate location on the face).
segments.purgatorio-32-g136: 'simile mostro' = 'such a monster'; 'visto ancor' = 'seen yet' with inversion; 'non fue' = passato remoto (non fu) 'had not been' — archaic past negation: 'such a monster had not yet been seen.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g137: 'Sicura' is an adjective used adverbially/predicatively ('secure/sure/securely placed') describing the position or aspect of the thing to follow; it heads an image compared in the next phrase.
segments.purgatorio-32-g138: 'quasi' = 'as if'; 'rocca' = 'fortress/stronghold'; 'in alto monte' = 'on a high mountain.' The phrase is a simile: 'secure, as if a fortress on a high mountain.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g139: Poetic inversion: 'seder sovresso' (literally 'to sit atop it') describes the posture; 'una puttana sciolta' is the subject 'a loose prostitute' (sciolta = unbound/dishevelled/free); 'm'apparve' = 'appeared to me' where the clitic 'm'' (mi) precedes the passato remoto 'apparve'; 'con le ciglia intorno pronte' = 'with the eyelashes all ready around' (an ablative/description).
segments.purgatorio-32-g140: 'e come' = 'and as/then as'; 'perché' here has a purposive/causal sense 'so that / as if so that'; 'non' negates; 'li' is a clitic pronoun (plural) referring to something earlier in the scene; 'fosse' is imperfect subjunctive of essere used in a subordinate purpose/result clause; 'tolta' is past participle 'taken away' agreeing (feminine) with the thing at risk. Grammatically: 'and (as if) so that she might not be taken (away) from them.'
segments.purgatorio-32-g141: 'vidi' = passato remoto 'I saw'; 'di costa' = 'on the slope/side'; 'a lei' = 'beside her/to her'; 'dritto' = 'straight/upright' (here: standing straight); 'un gigante' is the noun phrase 'a giant' appearing at the end by inversion for effect.
segments.purgatorio-32-g142: 'basciavansi' is an archaic/pronominal imperfect (or frequentative past) of 'baciare' with the clitic '-si' indicating reciprocal action: 'they kissed each other' or 'they would kiss one another'; 'insieme' = 'together'; 'alcuna volta' = 'at times/sometimes.'

sentences.purgatorio-32-s17: I did not hear it; nor is the hymn that those people then sang sung here, nor could I endure the whole of the note.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s18: If I could portray how the pitiless eyes grew drowsy at the sound of the Syrinx, the eyes for whom keeping watch had cost so dearly; like a painter who, working from a pattern, paints, I would draw how I fell asleep; but let whoever will have it suppose that the sleep was well feigned.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s19: Therefore I skip ahead to when I woke up, and I say that a brightness tore the veil of sleep from me, and a cry: "Rise — what are you doing?"
sentences.purgatorio-32-s1: So intent and fixed were my eyes on easing that ten-year thirst, that all my other senses were completely extinguished.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s2: And they, from here and from there, seemed not to abate — thus the holy smile drew them to itself among them with the old net!
sentences.purgatorio-32-s3: —; when by force my face was turned toward my left by those goddesses, for I heard from them a 'Too fixed!'
sentences.purgatorio-32-s4: ; and the disposition to see that the sun had just struck into my eyes, without my sight being somewhat restored to me.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s29: “Here you will be a sylvan for a short time;
sentences.purgatorio-32-s30: Therefore, for the good of a world that lives badly, keep your eyes now on the chariot; and what you see, when you return from there, see that you write it down.”
sentences.purgatorio-32-s31: Thus Beatrice; and I, who was wholly devoted at the feet of her commands, gave my mind and my eyes where she wished.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s32: Never did a fire descend from a thick cloud with so swift a motion, as when it rains from that boundary which lies furthest away; just so I saw the bird of Jove swoop down along the tree, breaking through the bark, and not sparing the flowers and the new leaves; and it struck the chariot with all its force; whereupon it bent like a ship in a storm, overcome by the wave, now by a swell, now by a luff.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s5: But after my sight was restored a little (and I say "a little" out of deference to how much I had been moved by a very perceptible impression that forced me to withdraw), I saw the glorious host turned upon my right arm and turn, with the sun and with the seven flames, toward the face.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s6: As a troop turns under its shields to protect itself, and turns about with the signal, before it can wholly change in itself; that military host of the heavenly kingdom which was advancing passed completely through before the chariot bent its first beam.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s7: Then the ladies turned to the wheels, and the griffin moved the blessed burden so that therefore not a single feather trembled.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s8: The beautiful lady who drew me to the passage — and Statius and I followed the wheel which had traced its orbit with a smaller arc.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s9: Thus, walking through the tall, empty wood, because of the fault of her who gave rise to the serpent, an angelic note tempered our steps.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s10: Perhaps in three flights a wild bolt covered as much distance as we were moved when Beatrice descended.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s11: I heard everyone murmur “Adam”; then they sought a tree stripped of leaves and of other foliage on each branch.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s12: Her lock of hair, which spreads out the more the higher it is, would (have been) seen by the Indians in their forests, admired for its height.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s25: Look at the company that surrounds her: the others, following the griffin, go upward with a sweeter and more profound song.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s26: And whether her speech was more extended, I do not know, because already, in my eyes, there was that one which had closed me to understanding other things.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s27: Alone she sits upon the true earth, like a guard left there of the wagon that I saw bound to the two-formed beast.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s28: The seven nymphs made for themselves a cloister in a circle, with those lamps in their hands that are safe from Aquilo and the South wind.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s13: “Blessed are you, griffin, who do not tear apart with your beak this wood, sweet to the taste, since thereby the belly is badly twisted.”
sentences.purgatorio-32-s14: Thus around the sturdy tree the others cried; and the two-bodied animal: “So is preserved the seed of every righteous man.”
sentences.purgatorio-32-s15: And having turned to the stem (or place) that he had pulled, he dragged it to the foot of the bereft bough, and he left that of hers tied to her.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s16: Like our plants: when down falls the great light, mixed with that which shines behind the heavenly gap, they become swollen, and afterward each one renews itself in its own color before the sun, with his steeds, comes under another star; showing a color less like roses and more like violets, the plant renewed itself which before had its boughs so sun-exposed.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s21: Just as, at the sight of the little blossoms on the apple-tree — which make the angels greedy for its fruit and cause perpetual nuptials in heaven — Peter and John and James, led and overcome, returned to the word from which their deeper sleep had been broken, and saw their school diminished both in respect of Moses and of Elijah, and their master's stole changed; so I turned back, and I saw that pious one standing above me who had before been the guide of my steps along the river.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s22: And, all in doubt, I said: "Where is Beatrice?"
sentences.purgatorio-32-s24: Thereupon she: “See her sitting beneath the fresh foliage upon her root.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s33: Then I saw a fox fling itself into the cradle of the triumphal vehicle, which seemed to be fasting from every good meal; but, reproving her for loathsome faults, my lady turned her into such a flight as bare bones without flesh could endure.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s34: Then, by the same way from which she had earlier come, I saw the eagle descend into the ark of the chariot and leave her feathered from itself; and, as when a heart laments, such a voice went out from heaven and thus said: 'O my little vessel, how ill‑laden you are!'
sentences.purgatorio-32-s36: Then it seemed to me that the earth opened between the two wheels, and I saw a dragon come out of it that fixed its tail up along the chariot; and like a wasp that draws back the needle, drawing its wicked tail toward itself, it pulled it up from the depths and lay there, wandering, wandering.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s41: But when the covetous, roaming eye turned toward me, that fierce paramour scourged her from head to foot; then, full of suspicion and harsh with anger, he loosed the monster and dragged it through the wood, so that she alone made me a shield against the prostitute and the new beast.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s37: That which remained, like ground made lively by couch-grass and by the feather — perhaps offered with a wholesome and kindly intention — covered itself again, and both wheel and axle were covered by it, to such a degree that the mouth can no longer hold a sigh.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s38: Thus transformed, the holy edifice put forth heads on its parts — three above the axle and one in each corner.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s39: The first ones were horned like oxen, but the four had a single horn on their foreheads; such a monster had not yet been seen.
sentences.purgatorio-32-s40: Secure, as if a fortress on a high mountain, a loose prostitute sat upon it and appeared to me with her eyelashes all ready around; and, as if so that she would not be taken from them, I saw a giant standing straight beside her on the slope; and they kissed together at times.
